Name: adaptive_spectral_activations
Title: Adaptive Spectral Activations for Latency-Aware Inference
Short Hypothesis: Can dynamically adjusting inference pathways based on real-time spectral activation profiles lead to improved latency and accuracy in low-latency machine learning applications?
Related Work: Existing methods like Spectral Quantization Routing and Training-Free Geometric Interference Cancellation primarily focus on static profiling or rely on heuristic methods. Our approach distinguishes itself by leveraging real-time spectral analysis to inform layer-skipping decisions, dynamically optimizing inference pathways without pre-training.
Abstract: We propose Adaptive Spectral Activations (ASA), a training-free framework that enhances low-latency inference by dynamically adjusting the pathways through a neural network based on the spectral properties of activations. Leveraging Jacobian-based layer-skipping, the ASA framework evaluates activation spectra in real-time, enabling the model to bypass layers that contribute minimal information based on spectral decay rates and covariance conditions. This method ensures strict adherence to latency budgets while maximizing throughput and accuracy. Our theoretical grounding in PAC-Bayes frameworks establishes formal risk bounds linking spectral properties to generalization stability. Initial experiments will validate the approach using standard benchmarks, comparing ASA's performance against traditional inference methods. Our results aim to demonstrate a superior throughput-accuracy Pareto frontier, showcasing ASA as a reproducible, robust solution for latency-critical applications.
Experiments: Implement ASA in a standard neural architecture and evaluate its performance on benchmark datasets.
Measure inference time, accuracy, and resource utilization under varying latency constraints.
Compare ASA against traditional methods (e.g., static layer execution) and dynamic routing approaches to validate improvements in throughput and accuracy.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Potential overfitting to specific input distributions if not generalized adequately.
Dependence on the accuracy of real-time spectral analysis, which may introduce computational overhead.
Limitations in the interpretability of dynamically adjusted pathways.
